entry.title: Re: PostgreSQL Schema Evolution in SAP CAP - How to Handl...
entry.content:
Thanks so much for the kind words.

Great question: in MTA, `@cds-pgroll/postgres` runs as a dedicated pre-deploy task/module, not a sidecar.
Flow is:

1) `pgroll-migrator` runs first (`node run-pgroll.js`)
2) `db-deployer` runs after it (`cds-deploy --model-only`)
3) `srv` starts last

In `mta.yaml`, this is enforced with `deployed-after` on the db-deployer.

So you keep a clean CI/CD pipeline with no manual SQL steps.

entry.title: Exploring Manufacturing in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602.1 (HFC6)
entry.content:
I'm excited to share the latest highlights for Manufacturing in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602.1 (HFC6). This release brings significant enhancements to Manufacturing and Production Planning, designed to provide production planners, supervisors, and supply chain managers with greater control, visibility, and efficiency. Lets explore the new capabilities that will help you streamline your operations.


Manage Material Coverage - Shipping Notification Quick View

In the Manage Material Coverage app (F0251A), we have introduced a new quick view for the MRP element Shipping Notification (ShpgNt). This enhancement provides planners with immediate access to critical information without navigating away from their main screen. The quick view displays details about the supplier, the responsible internal contact, and the confirmation status received from the supplier.

Manage Material Coverage - Shipping Notification Quick View

For deeper analysis, you can now navigate directly to the related purchase order from this view, significantly speeding up issue resolution and improving situational awareness.

 

Improved Stock Transfer Between MRP Areas

The overall stock transfer process between MRP areas is seen below. 

Stock Transfer Between MRP Areas

For what business use case do you need this new scenario?

The business case is that this new process allows you to have several storage location MRP areas (warehouses) now that are supplying your production warehouse.

Why do you need several source storage locations (warehouses)?

One of the reasons might be that you might want to have your supplier deliver raw materials or components to these source storage locations first, and then do a stock transfer from these source based locations to your production site or to your main warehouse in the production facility.  Meaning in SAP language that the raw materials or components that are stored in the source warehouses (source MRP area) are now needed in the target production facility (target MRP area). Also, another reason might be that you want to line up more than one source MRP area and then in the target MRP area you do the final assembly. 

Now with the latest release, the MRP can be used for the stock transfer of components from the warehouse (source MRP area) to the production facility (target MRP area) by either using a stock transfer reservation or requisition. 

Example: Stock transfer reservation

This feature improves the value of independent MRP areas and minimizes master data maintenance efforts, streamlining the planning process.


Capacity Scheduling Board

We have improved the user experience within the Capacity Scheduling Board. For better visibility and context during scheduling, you can now display the Product ID and Product Description directly in the Gantt chart. Simply access the Gantt Settings and select the Product ID and Product Description column to add it to your view:

View settings: Product ID and Product Description

 The result after selecting the above named settings looks like the following: 

Product ID and Product Description included as information

This removes the need to look up product details separately, making the scheduling process faster and more intuitive for your planners.

Manage Work Center Capacity

Another highlight is in the app Manage Work Center Capacity (F3289). Here we added the capability in the overview section to select the utilization with Maintenance as seen in the following screen: 

Utilization with Maintenance Settings

This gives the production planner not only the view of the utilization for planned or production orders, but also for maintenance orders to improve capacity planning on the respective Work Centers. 

Also, we included the maintenance order capacity information (in % and hours) in the table overview, so that the production planner can also use the grid view: 

Table with Maintenance Capacity View

 With this addition the maintenance order capacity value is now included in the utilization chart of the Manage Work Center Capacity Fiori app to give a holistic planning view to the production planner for their day to day work. 

SAP Cloud ERP: Manufacturing | Demos - How Tos 

Last but not least, I would like to introduce our new SAP Cloud ERP: Manufacturing  channel on YouTube to you. This new channel is embedded into the SAP Help and Learning YouTube channel which is your go-to destination for tutorials, product demos, and expert guidance on SAP solutions. Whether you’re getting started or looking to deepen your expertise, our videos cover setup, best practices, and step-by-step walkthroughs to help you succeed. If you have any topics where we need to create a Microlearning, please let us know. 

That's it and stay tuned - the next HFC is just around the corner. 

Best regards, 

Markus

entry.title: Re: Process Order BOM lists corrupted and cannot be corre...
entry.content:
From your post, it seems, you already checked with SAP who recommended a report and that did not solve your issue, my suggestion would be as follows:

  • Request SAP to validate whether Z_PPCON_124 handles scenarios where BOM components were changed after postings — it may not fully reset reservations if the BOM was incorrectly adjusted mid-process.
  • Ask if SAP has a standard correction program or OSS note for “negative withdrawn quantity” issues in process orders after BOM changes.
  • Provide technical details:
    • Process order numbers: 2502707799, 2502660794.
    • Current reservation status and component list mismatch.
    • Screenshots/logs showing negative withdrawal values.
  • Seek guidance on whether reservation re-creation (via CO02 or backend correction) is required instead of just running the correction report.
  • Confirm if SAP recommends a database-level adjustment or a specific note/patch to handle this scenario, since it seems beyond standard correction logic.

entry.title: Re: Moving Average Price is not getting updated
entry.content:
Hi Raju,

If your business process requires the price to be "picked up" from the Purchase Order automatically, Price Control S is technically the wrong setting.

* With Control S: The system insists on a pre-defined price. It will never let the PO dictate the material value. 

You should consider using price control V instead.

Best Regards,
Owen

entry.title: Re: SAP DM - how we empty the global variable
entry.content:
Hi,

there are two straightforward ways to empty a Global Variable in SAP Digital Manufacturing:

Option 1: Script Task

Add a Script Task to your production process and assign an empty value matching the variable's data type:

  • String: Global.myVariable = "";
  • Number: Global.myVariable = 0;
  • Boolean: Global.myVariable = false;
  • Object / complex type: Global.myVariable = null;

Note: Global Variables do not support expressions directly — the reset must be done inside a Script Task, not in a condition expression field.

Option 2: Write Global Variable Service Step

In the Production Process Designer, add a Write Global Variable service step to your automation sequence and set the value to "" (empty string) or 0 depending on the variable's data type. No scripting needed.

A few things to keep in mind:

  • Global Variables persist across process instances and are not automatically cleared when a process completes — you need to explicitly reset them as shown above.
  • There is no "delete" button in the UI; the approaches above simply overwrite the value with an empty/default state.
  • If you want to avoid stale data, a good practice is to place the reset step as the first step in your process so it always starts clean.

Hope this helps!

entry.title: Re: Moving Average Price is not getting updated
entry.content: We would like to inform you that we have enabled valuation category X at the header level. We have also maintained the price at the header level and assigned price control S at the segment level. Since SAP supports price control S for actual costing, we have entered the price in the purchase order. However, during GRN, the system is removing the price from the material master at the header level. As the valuation category is X, the system automatically generates the batch after GRN. Due to this behavior, it is not possible to create the batch and maintain the price before GRN.

I have requirement to generate multiple purchase requisitions base on reservations’ priorities as additional parameter. for example,

all 10  reservations have requirement date march 14, 2026. 3 reservations have priority 1, 4 reservations have priority 2, 3 reservations have priority 3. so i want to generate 3 purchase requisitions.

can you advise which enhancement can help to handle this scenario?

thanks,

Sajjad

entry.title: Re: Is transaction S_ALR_87005540 a reporting tcode?
entry.content:
Hi @Shivi_t 

The S_ALR_87005540 report is a standard SAP report that is part of the project information system, specifically designed for variance analysis in business processes.

It belongs to the Project System (PS) and Controlling (CO) modules.
It lists actual and target figures by business process group or individual processes.
It supports variance analysis based on Activity-Based Costing (ABC) variance analysis.
It structures the report according to variance categories to identify optimization potential.

Path: Information Systems → Project System → Financials → Costs → Plan-Based → Hierarchical → Plan/Actual/Variance

entry.title: Re: Embed self-hosted SAP Fiori Launchpad into Microsoft Teams
entry.content:
Hi @Martin-Pankraz,

clickjacking vulnerabilities are a serious threat and the protection against it must never be disabled!
Your reasoning has some gaps as the attack could be started from any web page inside the company firewall as well. The attack scenario could be an employee who tricks his manager to approve an authorization request by creating a malicious site in the company network. No firewall would be able to block this.

Therefore please rework this page and find a secure solution!
Possibly the missing part is described here: 3197303 - How to enable the custom html page to support SAP ClickJacking Framing Protection solution 

Best regards
Matthias

entry.title: Re: SAP PS Project system - display costs under maintenan...
entry.content:
Why costs stop at Order level is because, CN41N pulls costs from the WBS element hierarchy. When a maintenance order is assigned to a WBS element, the order costs are summarized and shown at the order node. The breakdown into internal labor, external services, stock/non‑stock material is not automatically displayed in CN41N because those are order cost line items, not WBS postings. Unless the order settlement has transferred costs into the WBS element, CN41N will show zeros below the order node.

Below are some of the options you can explore to see a detailed costs

1) Settlement of Orders
Ensure maintenance orders are settled to the WBS element. After settlement, CN41N can show costs at WBS level, but still aggregated — not broken down by cost category.

2) Use Order Cost Reports (PM/CO)
Transactions like IW33 → Costs tab, or KOB1/KOB3 (order line item reports) will show the detailed split: labor, services, materials. These are more precise for consumption analysis.

3) Custom Report / Info Structure
If you need CN41N to show the breakdown, you’d need an enhancement or a custom report that reads COEP/COBK (controlling line items) for the orders linked to WBS elements.

4) Standard PS Reports
Reports like CJI3 (Project Actual Cost Line Items) can show the detailed costs by cost element for the WBS, including postings coming from maintenance orders.

Having said that, the key takeaway is CN41N is designed for project hierarchy cost overview, not for detailed order cost analysis. That’s why you see zeros below the order level. To get the breakdown of internal labor, external services, stock/non‑stock materials, you’ll need to use order cost reports (PM/CO) or project line item reports (CJI3)

entry.title: SAP Generative AI Hub: Building AI-Powered CAP Applications on BTP (Part 5 of 6)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES: PART 5 OF 6  |  SAP BTP  |  CAP  |  AI CORE

SAP Generative AI Hub: Building AI-Powered CAP Applications on BTP

End-to-end architecture for AI-powered SAP extension applications using CAP — project setup, AI Core service binding, HANA Cloud persistence, Generative AI Hub integration in service handlers, SAP Integration Suite connectivity, and Cloud Foundry deployment.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Part 5 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: SAP CAP (Cloud Application Programming Model) is the standard framework for building SAP extension applications on BTP. When combined with AI Core and the Generative AI Hub, CAP becomes the production layer that exposes your AI pipelines as governed OData and REST APIs — consumable by S/4HANA, Fiori, Integration Suite, and any external system. This article covers the complete path: CAP project setup, AI Core service binding, HANA Cloud persistence, calling the Generative AI Hub from CAP service handlers, Integration Suite connectivity, and Cloud Foundry deployment — built around two production-ready use cases.

Series Context

This is Part 5 of a 6-part series on SAP Generative AI Hub. Parts 1–4 covered setup, Python SDK, prompt pipelines, and RAG on HANA Cloud vector store. This part moves from standalone Python scripts to production SAP applications — packaging the AI capabilities from previous parts into CAP services that integrate natively with the SAP ecosystem.

The RAG pipeline from Part 4 and the prompt pipelines from Part 3 are referenced throughout this article. If you are following the series end-to-end, those implementations carry forward directly into the CAP service handlers built here.

WHY CAP FOR SAP AI APPLICATIONS 

Why CAP Is the Right Production Layer for SAP AI

SAP CAP (Cloud Application Programming Model) is SAP's opinionated framework for building cloud-native extension applications on BTP. It handles OData protocol compliance, HANA Cloud schema management, BTP service binding, multi-tenancy, and authorization out of the box — letting you focus on business logic rather than infrastructure plumbing.

For SAP AI applications specifically, CAP provides three capabilities that matter in production:

  • Native BTP service binding — AI Core, HANA Cloud, and Destination Service credentials are injected automatically at runtime via VCAP_SERVICES, eliminating manual credential management
  • OData and REST API exposure — your AI pipelines become consumable APIs that S/4HANA, SAP Fiori, and SAP Integration Suite can call directly without custom integration code
  • HANA Cloud persistence — the same HANA Cloud instance that holds your vector store from Part 4 also persists CAP entities, keeping your entire data layer consolidated

The result is that the prompt pipelines and RAG architecture from earlier in this series stop being Python scripts and become governed, auditable, production-grade SAP services — with the access controls, monitoring, and deployment lifecycle that enterprise SAP landscapes require.

CAP is not a replacement for AI Core or the Generative AI Hub — it is the application layer that sits in front of them. AI Core handles model access, authentication, and governance. CAP handles API exposure, service binding, and SAP ecosystem integration. Both are required for production SAP AI applications.

ARCHITECTURE OVERVIEW 

End-to-End Architecture

The architecture for an AI-powered CAP application on BTP connects five layers:

S/4HANA / Fiori / Int. Suite → CAP OData/REST API → AI Core + Gen AI Hub →	HANA Cloud + Vector Store → Foundation Models

 

Layer

Role

S/4HANA / Fiori / Integration Suite

Consumers of the CAP API — send queries, receive AI-grounded responses

CAP Application (BTP CF)

OData and REST service layer — handles requests, calls AI Core, returns responses

SAP AI Core + Generative AI Hub

Foundation model access — GPT-4o, Claude, Gemini, Mistral via governed API

HANA Cloud

Dual purpose — CAP entity persistence AND vector store from Part 4

SAP Integration Suite

Orchestrates AI-augmented process flows across SAP and non-SAP systems

STEP 1: CAP PROJECT SETUP 

Setting Up the CAP Project

Prerequisites

Before initializing the CAP project, confirm the following are in place on your development machine:

  • Node.js 20 or higher — CAP requires a supported LTS version
  • @sap/cds-dk installed globally — the CAP development kit
  • Cloud Foundry CLI (cf) — for BTP deployment
  • SAP BTP subaccount with AI Core (Extended plan), HANA Cloud, and BTP CF runtime entitlements
  • AI Core resource group configured and service key available (covered in Part 1)

Initialize the CAP Project

# Install CAP development kit globally if not already installed
npm install -g @sap/cds-dk
 
# Initialize new CAP project
cds init ai-sap-service
cd ai-sap-service
 
# Install dependencies
npm install
 
# Add HANA Cloud support
cds add hana
 
# Add Cloud Foundry deployment configuration
cds add cf-manifest
 
# Install the SAP Generative AI Hub SDK
npm install @sap-ai-sdk/langchain @sap-ai-sdk/ai-api

The @sap-ai-sdk/langchain package is SAP's official JavaScript/TypeScript SDK for the Generative AI Hub — the Node.js equivalent of the generative-ai-hub-sdk used in Parts 2–4. It handles OAuth token management, deployment ID resolution, and model routing automatically within your CAP service handlers.

Project Structure

After initialization, your CAP project structure looks like this:

ai-sap-service/
├── db/
│   └── schema.cds          # HANA Cloud entity definitions
├── srv/
│   ├── ai-service.cds      # OData service definition
│   └── ai-service.js       # Service handler — AI logic lives here
├── app/                    # Optional Fiori UI
├── mta.yaml               # BTP deployment descriptor
├── package.json
└── .cdsrc.json            # CAP configuration

STEP 2: DATA MODEL AND SERVICE DEFINITION 

Defining the Data Model and OData Service

HANA Cloud Entity Schema (db/schema.cds)

Define the entities that CAP will deploy to HANA Cloud. These persist AI query history, knowledge base documents, and search results — enabling audit trails and caching for production workloads.

// db/schema.cds
namespace ai.sap.service;
 
entity AIQueryLog {
    key ID          : UUID;
    query           : String(2000);
    response        : LargeString;
    modelUsed       : String(100);
    sourceType      : String(50);   // 'MASTER_DATA' | 'KNOWLEDGE_BASE'
    confidenceScore : Decimal(4,3);
    createdAt       : Timestamp;
    createdBy       : String(100);
}
 
entity KnowledgeBaseDoc {
    key ID          : UUID;
    title           : String(500);
    content         : LargeString;
    category        : String(100);
    embeddingStatus : String(20);   // 'PENDING' | 'EMBEDDED' | 'FAILED'
    createdAt       : Timestamp;
}

OData Service Definition (srv/ai-service.cds)

Define the OData service that exposes your AI capabilities. CAP generates a fully compliant OData V4 API from this definition — no manual API implementation required.

// srv/ai-service.cds
using ai.sap.service from '../db/schema';
 
service AISapService @(path: '/ai-service') {
 
    // Expose query log as read-only OData entity
    @readonly
    entity QueryLog as projection on ai.sap.service.AIQueryLog;
 
    // Expose knowledge base docs with full CRUD
    entity KnowledgeDocs as projection on ai.sap.service.KnowledgeBaseDoc;
 
    // Custom action: AI-powered master data search
    action searchMasterData(query: String, maxResults: Integer)
        returns array of {
            materialNumber: String;
            description:   String;
            similarity:    Decimal;
            aiSummary:     String;
        };
 
    // Custom action: RAG-backed knowledge base Q&A
    action askKnowledgeBase(question: String, category: String)
        returns {
            answer:   String;
            sources:  array of { docId: String; title: String; score: Decimal; };
            modelUsed: String;
        };
}

CAP generates the full OData V4 metadata document, entity CRUD operations, and batch processing from the .cds definition automatically. The custom actions (searchMasterData, askKnowledgeBase) are where your AI logic runs — implemented in the service handler in the next step.

STEP 3: AI CORE SERVICE BINDING 

Binding AI Core and HANA Cloud to the CAP Application

BTP Service Instances

Create and bind the required BTP service instances to your CAP application. In BTP Cloud Foundry, service binding injects credentials into VCAP_SERVICES at runtime — the SAP AI SDK reads these automatically.

# Create AI Core service instance (Extended plan required for Gen AI Hub)
cf create-service aicore extended ai-core-instance
 
# Create HANA Cloud service instance
cf create-service hana-cloud hana ai-hana-instance
 
# Create Destination service instance (for Integration Suite connectivity)
cf create-service destination lite ai-destination-instance

MTA Deployment Descriptor (mta.yaml)

The mta.yaml file defines your CAP application's deployment configuration and service bindings for BTP. CAP generates a base version with cds add cf-manifest — extend it to include AI Core binding:

# mta.yaml — key sections for AI Core binding
modules:
  - name: ai-sap-service-srv
    type: nodejs
    path: gen/srv
    parameters:
      buildpack: nodejs_buildpack
      memory: 512M
    requires:
      - name: ai-core-instance      # AI Core binding
      - name: ai-hana-instance      # HANA Cloud binding
      - name: ai-destination-instance
 
resources:
  - name: ai-core-instance
    type: org.cloudfoundry.managed-service
    parameters:
      service: aicore
      service-plan: extended
  - name: ai-hana-instance
    type: org.cloudfoundry.managed-service
    parameters:
      service: hana-cloud
      service-plan: hana
  - name: ai-destination-instance
    type: org.cloudfoundry.managed-service
    parameters:
      service: destination
      service-plan: lite

The AI Core Extended plan is required for Generative AI Hub access. The Standard plan provides AI Core infrastructure for custom model training but does not include Generative AI Hub model access. Verify your BTP subaccount has the Extended plan entitlement before deploying.

STEP 4: AI LOGIC IN CAP SERVICE HANDLERS 

Implementing AI Logic in CAP Service Handlers

The service handler file (srv/ai-service.js) is where your AI logic runs. CAP routes incoming OData action calls to the handler functions registered here. The SAP AI SDK handles AI Core authentication automatically using the VCAP_SERVICES credentials injected at runtime.

  ▶  Use Case 1: AI-Powered Master Data Search 

searchMasterData Action Handler

This handler receives a natural language search query, converts it to a vector embedding using the SAP embedding model, retrieves similar master data records from the HANA vector store built in Part 4, then generates an AI summary for each result. The response is returned as a structured OData collection.

// srv/ai-service.js
const cds = require('@sap/cds');
const { ChatOpenAI } = require('@sap-ai-sdk/langchain');
const { OpenAIEmbeddings } = require('@sap-ai-sdk/langchain');
 
module.exports = cds.service.impl(async function() {
 
    // Initialize AI models — SDK reads AI Core credentials from VCAP_SERVICES
    const llm = new ChatOpenAI({
        modelName: 'gpt-4o',
        resourceGroup: 'default'
    });
 
    const embeddings = new OpenAIEmbeddings({
        modelName: 'text-embedding-ada-002',
        // SAP embedding model via AI Core
        resourceGroup: 'default'
    });
 
    // Use Case 1: AI-powered master data search
    this.on('searchMasterData', async (req) => {
        const { query, maxResults = 5 } = req.data;
 
        // Step 1: Generate embedding for the search query
        const queryEmbedding = await embeddings.embedQuery(query);
 
        // Step 2: Similarity search in HANA vector store
        const db = await cds.connect.to('db');
        const results = await db.run(`
            SELECT TOP ${maxResults}
                SOURCE_ID as materialNumber,
                CHUNK_TEXT as description,
                COSINE_SIMILARITY(EMBEDDING, TO_REAL_VECTOR('${JSON.stringify(queryEmbedding)}'))
                AS similarity
            FROM AI_VECTOR_STORE.DOCUMENT_EMBEDDINGS
            WHERE SOURCE_TYPE = 'MASTER_DATA'
            ORDER BY similarity DESC
        `);
 
        // Step 3: Generate AI summary for each result
        const enriched = await Promise.all(results.map(async (row) => {
            const summary = await llm.invoke([{
                role: 'system',
                content: 'Summarize this SAP material record in one sentence. Be specific and technical.'
            }, {
                role: 'user',
                content: `Material: ${row.description}`
            }]);
 
            return {
                materialNumber: row.materialNumber,
                description:   row.description,
                similarity:    parseFloat(row.similarity.toFixed(4)),
                aiSummary:     summary.content
            };
        }));
 
        // Step 4: Log query to HANA for audit trail
        await db.run(INSERT.into('ai.sap.service.AIQueryLog').entries({
            ID:             cds.utils.uuid(),
            query,
            response:       JSON.stringify(enriched),
            modelUsed:      'gpt-4o',
            sourceType:     'MASTER_DATA',
            createdAt:      new Date().toISOString(),
            createdBy:      req.user?.id || 'system'
        }));
 
        return enriched;
    });

  ▶  Use Case 2: RAG-Backed Knowledge Base Q&A 

askKnowledgeBase Action Handler

This handler implements the full RAG pipeline from Part 4 inside a CAP service action — making it callable as an OData action from S/4HANA, Fiori, or Integration Suite. The category parameter enables scoped retrieval, limiting similarity search to a specific knowledge domain.

    // Use Case 2: RAG-backed knowledge base Q&A
    this.on('askKnowledgeBase', async (req) => {
        const { question, category } = req.data;
 
        // Step 1: Embed the question
        const questionEmbedding = await embeddings.embedQuery(question);
 
        // Step 2: Retrieve relevant chunks from HANA vector store
        const db = await cds.connect.to('db');
        const categoryFilter = category ? `AND JSON_VALUE(METADATA, '$.category') = '${category}'` : '';
 
        const chunks = await db.run(`
            SELECT TOP 5
                SOURCE_ID as docId,
                CHUNK_TEXT as content,
                METADATA,
                COSINE_SIMILARITY(EMBEDDING, TO_REAL_VECTOR('${JSON.stringify(questionEmbedding)}'))
                AS score
            FROM AI_VECTOR_STORE.DOCUMENT_EMBEDDINGS
            WHERE SOURCE_TYPE = 'KB'
            AND COSINE_SIMILARITY(EMBEDDING, TO_REAL_VECTOR('${JSON.stringify(questionEmbedding)}')) > 0.75
            ${categoryFilter}
            ORDER BY score DESC
        `);
 
        // Step 3: Assemble retrieved context
        const context = chunks.map(c =>
            `[Source: ${c.docId} | Score: ${c.score.toFixed(3)}]\n${c.content}`
        ).join('\n\n---\n\n');
 
        // Step 4: Generate grounded answer — ONLY from retrieved context
        const answer = await llm.invoke([{
            role: 'system',
            content: `You are an SAP expert assistant.
Answer questions using ONLY the context provided.
If the context does not contain enough information, say so explicitly.
Do not use general SAP knowledge — only the provided context.
Always reference the source document when citing specific information.`
        }, {
            role: 'user',
            content: `CONTEXT:\n${context}\n\nQUESTION: ${question}`
        }]);
 
        // Step 5: Build response with sources
        const sources = await Promise.all(chunks.map(async (c) => {
            const doc = await SELECT.one.from('ai.sap.service.KnowledgeBaseDoc')
                .where({ ID: c.docId });
            return {
                docId: c.docId,
                title: doc?.title || c.docId,
                score: parseFloat(c.score.toFixed(4))
            };
        }));
 
        // Step 6: Log to HANA for audit
        await db.run(INSERT.into('ai.sap.service.AIQueryLog').entries({
            ID:             cds.utils.uuid(),
            query:          question,
            response:       answer.content,
            modelUsed:      'gpt-4o',
            sourceType:     'KNOWLEDGE_BASE',
            createdAt:      new Date().toISOString(),
            createdBy:      req.user?.id || 'system'
        }));
 
        return {
            answer:    answer.content,
            sources,
            modelUsed: 'gpt-4o'
        };
    });
 
});

Every AI query is logged to HANA via the AIQueryLog entity. This audit trail is not optional in enterprise SAP deployments — it provides the traceability that compliance, security, and governance teams require. The log captures the query, response, model used, source type, timestamp, and user — everything needed for a complete audit record.

STEP 5: SAP INTEGRATION SUITE CONNECTIVITY 

Connecting via SAP Integration Suite

SAP Integration Suite acts as the orchestration layer between your CAP AI service and SAP backend systems. Rather than S/4HANA calling your CAP service directly, Integration Suite manages the flow — applying transformation, error handling, retry logic, and monitoring across the end-to-end process.

Destination Configuration

Create a BTP Destination pointing to your deployed CAP service. Integration Suite uses this destination to route calls to your AI endpoints:

# BTP Destination configuration for CAP AI service
Name:             AI_SAP_SERVICE
Type:             HTTP
URL:              https://ai-sap-service-srv.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com
Authentication:   OAuth2ClientCredentials
Client ID:        <CAP application client ID>
Token Service URL: https://<subdomain>.authentication.<region>.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token

Integration Suite iFlow Pattern

A typical AI-augmented process flow in Integration Suite follows this pattern — S/4HANA triggers a business event, Integration Suite calls your CAP AI service, the AI response enriches or routes the downstream process:

S/4HANA event →	iFlow trigger →	CAP AI action call → AI response → Process routing

Example use case: A new material is created in S/4HANA. Integration Suite captures the MaterialCreated event, calls the CAP searchMasterData action to check for similar existing materials, and routes the process based on the AI similarity score — flagging potential duplicates for review before the material is activated.

When calling CAP OData actions from Integration Suite iFlows, use the HTTP Receiver adapter with OAuth2 authentication pointing to the BTP Destination created above. Do not embed AI Core credentials directly in iFlow configurations — all AI credential management should remain within the CAP service layer and its BTP service bindings.

STEP 6: CLOUD FOUNDRY DEPLOYMENT 

Deploying to BTP Cloud Foundry

Build and Deploy

# Build the CAP project for production
cds build --production
 
# Build the MTA archive
mbt build
 
# Deploy to BTP Cloud Foundry
cf login -a https://api.cf.<region>.hana.ondemand.com
cf deploy mta_archives/ai-sap-service_1.0.0.mtar

Verify Deployment

# Check application status
cf apps
 
# Check service bindings are correctly attached
cf services
 
# Tail logs to confirm AI Core connection on startup
cf logs ai-sap-service-srv --recent

Test the Deployed OData Actions

Once deployed, test your AI actions via the OData endpoint. The CAP service exposes a metadata document at /ai-service/$metadata — verify it loads correctly before testing the actions:

# Test master data search action
curl -X POST 'https://ai-sap-service-srv.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com/ai-service/searchMasterData' \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"query": "hydraulic pump assembly", "maxResults": 3}'
 
# Test knowledge base Q&A action
curl -X POST 'https://ai-sap-service-srv.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com/ai-service/askKnowledgeBase' \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"question": "How should I handle delta load failures?", "category": "HANA"}'

A successful deployment produces two working OData actions callable from any SAP system or external client — AI-powered master data search and RAG-backed knowledge base Q&A — with full audit logging to HANA Cloud, governed AI Core authentication, and Integration Suite connectivity for S/4HANA process orchestration.

PRODUCTION DESIGN CONSIDERATIONS 

What to Get Right Before Going Live

 

Consideration

Guidance

AI Core credentials

Never hardcode credentials in CAP service handlers. Always use VCAP_SERVICES binding via mta.yaml. Hardcoded credentials are a security violation and will fail rotation.

HANA vector store alignment

The HANA Cloud instance bound to your CAP application must be the same instance containing the AI_VECTOR_STORE schema from Part 4. Mismatched instances is the most common deployment error.

Similarity score threshold

Keep the 0.75 COSINE_SIMILARITY threshold from Part 4 in your CAP handler. Removing it returns low-relevance chunks that degrade answer quality and increase token consumption.

Audit log privacy

The AIQueryLog entity captures user queries. Review your data privacy policy before deploying — in some organizations, logging user query content requires explicit data handling disclosure.

Integration Suite authentication

iFlows calling your CAP service must use OAuth2 via BTP Destination — not basic auth. Basic auth against CAP OData endpoints is deprecated in BTP Cloud Foundry environments.

CAP multitenancy

If your CAP application serves multiple SAP tenants, enable CAP's built-in multitenancy (cds add multitenancy) before deploying. Single-tenant deployments cannot be retrofitted for multitenancy without significant rework.

What's Next in This Series

  • Part 6: Extending SAP Joule with Foundation Models — custom Joule skills backed by AI Core pipelines, with HANA vector store retrieval for grounded natural language interaction across SAP applications

The CAP service built in this article is the foundation for the Joule extension in Part 6. The OData actions defined here — searchMasterData and askKnowledgeBase — are called directly from the Joule skill implementation in the final part of this series. Keep your deployed CAP service running as you move to Part 6.

entry.title: Get Help Where You Need it: SAP Companion in SAP Asset Performance Management
entry.content:
Access relevant help, documentation, and guidance directly within your workflow
Daily work in SAP Asset Performance Management often involves navigating different applications, reviewing information, and completing operational tasks. During this process, users sometimes want quick clarification about a feature, an application purpose, or the next step in a workflow.

Now imagine being able to access that guidance without leaving the application you are working in. The information appears within the same screen, aligned with the application you are currently using, allowing you to continue your work while exploring helpful context.

This is where SAP Companion adds value. It brings contextual guidance directly into the application interface so that users can access relevant information exactly when they need it.

What is SAP Companion

SAP Companion is an in-application assistance feature that provides contextual help within SAP applications.
The guidance appears as a help panel overlay on top of the active application screen, offering relevant guidance based on the page or application you are currently using.
This approach supports a natural learning experience because users can learn while they work, accessing information exactly when they need it.

Why Contextual Help Is Valuable

Providing help in the right context improves the user experience and reduces the effort required to find information. SAP Companion in SAP Asset Performance Management offers several benefits:
  • Context-Sensitive Help: Users receive information relevant to the task or application they are currently working on.
  • Stay in Your Workflow: Help appears directly within the interface, so users do not need to navigate away from the application.
  • Faster Onboarding: New users can quickly understand application functionality while performing their daily tasks.
  • Improved Productivity: Users can resolve questions instantly without searching through large documentation portals.

Accessing SAP Companion

Accessing contextual help is simple.

  1. Open the Fiori Launchpad.

  2. In the launchpad header, select the Help icon.

  3. The help panel opens and displays contextual information related to your current page or application.

The help panel appears as an overlay on your current screen, allowing you to continue working while accessing relevant guidance.

Help icon in the application header used to access in-app assistance (SAP companion).

Information Available in the Help Panel

The help panel offers several types of information designed to support users directly within their workflow.

  • Contextual Snippets: Short descriptions introduce the purpose and main functionality of the application. These snippets provide quick orientation and help users understand what the application is designed for.
  • Direct Links to Documentation: Each application includes a link that leads directly to its guide in the SAP Help Portal, allowing users to explore detailed documentation whenever needed.
  • Technical Component Details: The help panel also displays the technical application component identifier. This information can be useful when creating support tickets or communicating with support teams.

Contextual help snippet showing the explanatory text, a link to documentation, and the technical component ID.

Watch the Demo

To see how contextual help works in practice, watch the video below:

 
Video demonstrating how to access in-app assistance.

Guidance that appears directly within the application interface creates a more connected learning experience for users. 

With SAP Companion, contextual assistance becomes part of the user experience in SAP Asset Performance Management, ensuring that help is always available exactly where it is needed.

By delivering the right information at the right moment, SAP Companion helps users work more efficiently and confidently within the application.

For detailed information about the application and how to use its feature, refer to the application guide:
SAP Asset Performance Management | SAP Help Portal

To help shape future enhancements, you can share your feedback and vote for features through the SAP Customer Influence Portal. Your input directly contributes to improving the experience.

entry.title: Re: Grouping of Reservations into Purchase Requisition ba...
entry.content:
In standard ECC MRP logic, when lot size = EX (exact lot size) and requirement dates are identical, the system groups multiple reservations into a single purchase requisition. To split them based on an additional parameter like priority, you will need to use an enhancement point in the MRP run.

So what you can do is add a priority field to reservations (either custom field or classification). In BAdI MD_PURREQ_POST, read the reservation priority and enforce splitting logic. If multiple reservations share the same date but have different priorities, create separate PRs for each priority group. Ensure that the PR text or custom field carries the reservation priority so downstream users can identify it.

Most importantly, you will need to test carefully to avoid excessive PR creation (e.g., one PR per reservation instead of per priority group). If priorities are business‑critical, consider whether they should be modeled as different MRP groups or lot‑sizing procedures instead of custom enhancement.

entry.title: How to enforce a Maximum Stock Level per MRP Area (IT_SGF) – physical storage constraint scenario
entry.content:
Hello experts,

We’re running S/4HANA and facing a business requirement to limit replenishment at MRP Area level due to a physical storage constraint.

Scenario:

  • Material: XEE.A0879.1
  • MRP Area: IT_SGF
  • Storage location: SGS1 (limited capacity)
  • Automatic replenishment handled by ZMM_DISPATCH (converts PRs → STOs)

We’d like to ensure that once the available stock within IT_SGF MRP Area reaches a defined maximum, no new MRP proposals or automatic STO conversions are triggered — even if new demand exists.

We understand that in standard MRP, the Maximum Stock Level field doesn’t act as a hard stop and that MRP always covers demand. However, since this requirement derives from physical storage capacity, not planning logic, we’re trying to identify:

  • Any standard or configuration-based approach to cap stock within an MRP Area, or
  • A BAdI/enhancement or best practice to enforce such a rule, possibly in the MRP or ZMM_DISPATCH process, or
  • Whether SAP recommends addressing this via EWM capacity checks instead of MRP logic.

Has anyone implemented or advised on a similar setup?

Any reference to OSS notes, BAdIs, or best practices would be highly appreciated.

Thanks in advance,

Paolo

entry.title: Re: Issue with Indirect PR Release Strategy & Workflow fo...
entry.content:
You have already done the right checks — CT04, CL02, CL24N, transports — so the fact that AED works in DEV but not in QAS/PRD points to a classification value or buffer inconsistency rather than a configuration gap. Here are some focused areas to check further:

Below are the investigation steps you can try

1) Characteristic Value Transport
Verify that the currency characteristic (e.g., CEKKO-WAERS) includes AED in QAS/PRD. Sometimes the value range or allowed entries are system‑specific and not transported. Run CT04 in QAS/PRD and confirm AED is explicitly listed.

2) Classification Assignment
In CL24N, check that the release strategy class has AED correctly assigned in QAS/PRD. Even if the class exists, the individual value assignment may not have been transported.

3) Check CEKKO/CEBAN Runtime Values
Use SE16N to look at table CEBAN for the PR in question. Confirm that the currency field (WAERS) is populated with AED exactly as expected. If the runtime value does not match the classification entry (e.g., spacing, case sensitivity, missing entry), release strategy won’t be determined.

4) Buffer/Classification Regeneration
Run program RCCTBI01 (Classification consistency check) or report RCCTBI02 to rebuild classification buffers. Sometimes after transport, classification tables are not refreshed, leading to mismatches.

5) Workflow vs. Release Strategy
The fact that workflow triggers means the event linkage is fine. The missing approver in the Release Strategy tab is purely a strategy determination issue. This confirms the problem is in classification values, not workflow configuration

entry.title: Re: SAP ECC - sales order with item and schedule line not...
entry.content:
You see CustSt because the goods are managed in sales order stock. You don’t see CustOrd because SAP avoids double-counting and the requirement is already represented by the customer stock element.

If your business process requires visibility of both (sales order + schedule line as CustOrd), you would need to adjust the requirement type or schedule line category so that it doesn’t create sales order stock (e.g., using a requirement type like KAT instead of KEB). That way, MD04 would show the "CustOrd" element separately.

entry.title: Re: CDN Commerce API with the Composable Storefront OCC_B...
entry.content:
Hi,

Please check the help page: Add Applications to JavaScript Storefronts. OCC_BACKEND_BASE_URL_VALUE placeholder is just one approach that can be used. It works well with blue / green deployments. Another approach than can be used is to define a set of properties ccv2.occ.backend.base.url.<$jsapp>.value (e.g. ccv2.occ.backend.base.url.spartacusstore.value=https://my.cdn.api.url/) for each of the applications. This can be done in Cloud Portal as described on help page Service Properties. However when using this approach you won't be able to use the benefits of blue / green deployments.

entry.title: MM LPA Scheduling Agreement and metal product pricing
entry.content:
Hello there, we have had the challenge of using MM LPA Scheduling Agreement for metal product in price discrepancy between supplier sent invoice price for JIT GR and the system pulls price for JIT GR from the condition. 

Below is describing out situation and current challenge.

  1. We use the MM LPA Scheduling Agreement functionality for the metal product purchase.
  2. SAP standard MM LPA is designed to use material GR date-based price that is pick from the time-dependent condition for matching AP invoice price when vendor invoice is being posted.
  3. The actual Metal (market) prices fluctuate quite often such as every day and when AP invoice is being processed against GRed qty, the net price which suppliers sent in their invoice does not match LPA condition that is maintained in SAP.  This results in price discrepancy and the invoice is blocked.
  4. We do not want to use discrete po which we used to use for this purchase because Scheduling Agreement has more benefit in metal product purchasing process than stand-alone PO except the problem above. 

We are wondering if there any other SAP customers that have the same/similar challenge in LPA/SA and how to manage it.  Could we have any input/insights on this? thank you.

entry.title: Video Series for Customers Converting their SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2SL to 3SL
entry.content:
We’re pleased to introduce a new series of concise, practical videos designed to support customers transitioning from a 2‑system to a 3‑system landscape in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. The videos highlight the benefits, key considerations, and what to expect during the conversion journey.

Here you can find the links to the videos:

  • Managing Transports in SAP S/4HANA Cloud 3-System Landscape: This video describes the transport management processes for different types of data from Development, to Test, to Production. The transport process is organized, streamlined, and reduces risk when using the 3-System landscape.
  • Exploring the Test Automation Tool in SAP S/4HANA Cloud 3-System Landscape: This video describes the features of the Test Automation Tool to simplify business process testing after release upgrades. The Test Automation Tool can also be used when a Parallel Line is generated to create a secondary test tenant. Customers receive the most value from the Test Automation Tool when used in a 3-System landscape.
  • Navigating Parallel Line in SAP S/4HANA Cloud 3-System Landscape: This video describes the features of the Parallel Line, which can be generated as a copy of your main line to provide an additional SAP Central Business Configuration workspace, Development tenant, and Test tenant for a variety of different use cases. This feature has no cost and is only available in 3-System landscape.
  • Exploring Developer Extensibility in SAP S/4HANA Cloud 3-System Landscape: This video highlights three different types of extensibility: Key User, Developer, and Side-by-Side. While Key User and Side-by-Side extensibility are available in both 2 and 3-System landscape, Developer extensibility is only available in 3-System landscape. Developer extensibility provides a unique opportunity to create customer-specific extensions to released SAP objects with ABAP code, providing a deeper layer of customization.
  • Using the Configuration Localization Tool in SAP S/4HANA Cloud 3-System Landscape: This video describes the features of the Configuration Localization Tool to create customer-specific countries, which is only available in 3-System landscape.

If you want to learn how to implement SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and get certified, please refer to this learning journey: Implementing SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

entry.title: Re: Are SAP projects still too customization-heavy?
entry.content: In my experience it’s a mix. A lot of projects say they want to follow Clean Core principles, but once business requirements start piling up, teams often customize anyway — especially for reporting or workflow quirks that standard tools don’t handle out of the box. The ideal (and most sustainable) outcome is to push as much as possible into configuration before resorting to customization, but in reality it’s often somewhere in between. The key is always asking “can this be done without code?” before making changes.

entry.title: Flexible ABAP - a new JetBrains plugin for ABAP, CDS, and BDEF
entry.content:
Hey everybody,

I just launched a new JetBrains plugin: "Flexible ABAP". It works for all JetBrains IDEs (IntelliJ, PyCharm, WebStorm, CLion etc) and you can find it on the Marketplace by searching "Flexible ABAP" (happy if someone wants to link it, I never know the etiquette on these forums).

Here's a quick few screenshots:

 

 

 

 

 

My own ABAP days date back to the early 2000s, when I got to work on some Fraport machines. I loved the language. Now, years later, I wanted to get back into it (and rerun some projects of mine) and looked for IDE integrations. I noticed that the only one I could find was still for Eclipse. I do language implementations for fun in my spare time, so I figured I may have something to contribute to the community.

Anyway, since neither the ABAP language spec nor existing grammar implementations were publicly available, I had to recreate the whole thing from scratch. Full BNF grammar for ABAP, BDEF and CDS views. It was a lot of work but also the kind of thing I find weirdly enjoyable. The benefit of having done this is that now I can do pretty great things in the IDE - refactoring, on-hover documentation, auto-complete, code validation, structured views etc all came in pretty quickly.

Even though it is new, I tried to ship with a useful set of features out of the gate: autocomplete for all three languages, hover docs with SAP Help links, various live templates (they make it faster to write snippets),  postfix templates, structure view, code inspections with quick fixes, and proper themes for all three languages. I also added ways for you to install the sdk or start run/debug sessions. The foundation is there.

With your help, I now want to turn it into a really great environment for ABAP developers. I'm really early though and since the grammar was built without a spec to lean on, your feedback matters more than usual. What works, what breaks, what I got wrong. I'm happy to hand out free voucher codes for a trial month to anyone who wants to give it a go and share their experience. Just reply here or reach out on GitHub and I'll send one over.

On pricing: free for open source projects and students, 50% off for non-profits and startups. But if none of that applies, just reach out anyway. Right now I care about getting this right more than anything else.

 

Would love to hear your thoughts!

Cheers, Paul

entry.title: Re: Event Mesh + AMQP: The Retry Trap Nobody Warns You About
entry.content:
@MateuszPiotrowski 
Do you think that this can treated as a Cons for Event Mesh? 
Actually, I do not know how can this Event Mesh error handling work in Production instances.

So in this case it would require for each event to have a dedicated queue, a dedicated retry queue, a dedicated DMQ queue, and a dedicated separate iFlow for reprocessing. Is this right?
Otherwise, if one queue serves multiple events ( which have separate consumer iFlows ) and if there are errors for multiple events ( saved into the same DMQ), those can only be reprocessed if all the other events get processed again, reproducing several unwanted errors again.

Does this described the possible retry process for Event Mesh, or is there a better way to reprocess individual messages? Or resending multiple failed events, just to resend one individual message is something that does not need to be worried. 

Appreciate your answer.

entry.title: Calculating Manager input in the comment section for CDP
entry.content:
We are working on a People Analytics Story Report for CY 2026 CDP and need assistance with calculating the “Manager Input” metric.

Business Definition

Manager Input (overall) = 1 if the manager has left a comment in at least one section of the CDP; otherwise 0.

The final KPI should represent the % of manager input in CDP,

Current Setup

  • I have created section‑level manager comment flags (0/1) per section, for example: Manager_Comment_My Current Role Manager_Comment_Area of Strength etc.
  • Each flag correctly shows 1 when a manager comment exists for that section and 0 otherwise.
  • I then created an overall manager input flag using logic such as: IF(ISNULL([M_C]), 0, [M_C]) + IF(ISNULL([M_C_1]), 0, [M_C_1]) + IF(ISNULL([M_C_2]), 0, [M_C_2]) + IF(ISNULL([M_C_3]), 0, [M_C_3])

Issue

When I attempt to calculate the total number of comment with manager input, I get unexpected results: In some cases, the total inflates significantly (e.g., 168 instead of 4), which appears to be caused by row duplication from section/comment‑level data.

All calculation I added on query level, in calculated comment

Does anyone have any suggestion how to fix it?

entry.title: Free course : Introducing SAP Business Data Cloud - available in multiple languages
entry.content:
SAP Business Data Cloud

SAP Business Data Cloud is a data integration and management platform designed to help organizations connect, manage, and activate their business data. Here are its key aspects:

Core Capabilities

  • Data Integration: Connects data from multiple SAP and non-SAP sources
  • Master Data Management: Creates a single source of truth for business entities (customers, products, suppliers, etc.)
  • Data Quality: Cleanses, deduplicates, and enriches data
  • Data Activation: Makes data available to business applications and analytics tools

Key Features

Feature Purpose

Reduced complexityData management landscape by using fewer technologies and tools
UnifiedBusiness view of their data
Trusted and SecureData foundation that they can use to build reliable AI applications
ConnectorsPre-built integrations with SAP and third-party systems
AnalyticsProvides insights into data quality and usage

Common Use Cases

  1. Customer 360: Single view of all customer information
  2. Product Data Governance: Consistent product information across channels
  3. Supplier Management: Unified supplier information
  4. Data Compliance: GDPR and regulatory data management

Target Users

  • Large enterprises with multiple data sources
  • Organizations needing centralized data governance
  • Companies requiring data quality and master data management

Key Course Topics and what you'll learn:

Introducing SAP Business Data Cloud
Discovering the Data Engineering Components of SAP Business Data Cloud
Discovering the Data Analysis Components of SAP Business Data Cloud

When you complete the course, you will receive your SAP Badge Learning.

SAP Badge Learning refers to SAP's digital badging system that recognizes and validates professional skills and knowledge achievements. 

Official SAP Training Platform: 
Supported Languages:
English
German
French
Spanish
Portuguese
Japanese
Chinese
Korean

Benefits

✓ Career development and skill validation ✓ Employer recognition ✓ Structured learning paths ✓ Industry-recognized credentials

Have a good learning journey students

Team Analytics

entry.title: Increasing Sales Performance: Why aren’t we talking about the cognitive load of sales travel?
entry.content:
Why aren’t we talking about the cognitive load of sales travel?

I travel a lot for work. In fact, I’m writing this from an aisle seat, drinking mediocre airline coffee. And while I have my packing system down to a science, the amount of mental effort that goes into booking travel is mind boggling. The plethora of small decisions – flight time, airline, hotel cost vs proximity to the office – add to the big ones, like which trips are worth taking because they drive business forward. It takes a lot of mental effort that could have been spent doing much more impactful work.

We spend so much time trying to crack the code of sales productivity. And we should. Non-selling activities still consume 2/3 of the average sales team’s time, according to McKinsey. What if we’re so focused on time savings that we’re overlooking the importance of protecting another precious resource: our sellers’ cognitive power? There’s an obvious productivity killer staring us right in the face:  constant micro-decisions unrelated to selling. These continual small choices might seem insignificant, but in reality when added up, come at a high cost in relation to the mental toll, taxing sales reps unnecessarily each day. .

Context Switching: The Silent Tax on Productivity

Today’s sales landscape requires continual context-switching. Our sellers jump from researching customers to trainings about new products to back-to-back customer calls. Modern CRMs like SAP Sales Cloud keep sellers focused and productive.; we’ve come a long way from the “swivel chair” concept. But to help our sellers be more productive and effective, decreasing context switching and the associated mental stain is critical.

My Product Marketing team just tried an exercise to illustrate the cognitive load of context switching:

  1. First, count aloud by 2s until you reach 20. Easy.
  2. Then, spell the word “strawberry” aloud. Also easy.
  3. Now, alternate one letter and one number: 2-S-4-T-6-R, and so forth. It’s embarrassingly difficult.

If selling is one activity, and booking travel is another, you can imagine how hard a rep’s brain is working during that switch, even if the rep isn't aware of it. But that productivity loss adds up in a big way. There’s a better way: making the task of booking travel as minimally taxing as possible thanks to a little help from our very smart friend:

Offloading Mental Effort, Ramping Up Sales

SAP thinks about these challenges just like you do, because we have sellers and sales leaders we care about, too. We’re integrating Booking Agent into SAP Sales Cloud, so now sellers can use Joule to book travel to visit their clients, directly from the screen where they’re working their deal.

Joule uses the details of the opportunity to create itinerary options for the salesperson to choose from. They can book right there by clicking a button, or they can tell Joule what they want to change. Joule handles all those micro-decisions, freeing up your seller so they can focus on selling.

When you challenge yourself to think differently about what you can do to make space for your sellers to be brilliant, you come to realize that eliminating unnecessary effort is what modern productivity looks like. And yeah, this saves time too.

(Booking Agent within SAP Sales Cloud Generally Available in Q2 2026)
